metadata.summary: Install Ollama, manage local LLM models, and select the right model for RAM/VRAM constraints. Covers setup, model catalog, hardware requirements, and API endpoints.

content.title: Ollama Setup and Model Management
content.summary: Ollama is an open-source platform for running LLMs locally. It bundles model weights, configuration, and data into a single Modelfile package (similar to Docker). Supports NVIDIA (CUDA), Apple Silicon (Metal), and AMD (ROCm) GPUs. Default endpoint: http://localhost:11434.

why.p: Running LLMs locally eliminates external API calls for data privacy requirements (HIPAA, GDPR, corporate policy), enables offline and air-gapped environments, removes API costs for high-volume tasks, and allows custom fine-tuned model deployment. Correct model selection relative to available RAM/VRAM is the single most critical decision — a model that doesn't fit in VRAM falls back to CPU with 5-20x slower inference.

list.item: Data privacy requirements where no external API calls are permitted.
Offline or air-gapped environments (IoT, edge, secure networks).
High-volume classification or extraction tasks where zero marginal API cost matters.
Development and testing — no API keys, no rate limits, no costs, no internet required.
Custom fine-tuned models that must be deployed locally after training.
Best-quality responses required — even largest local models (70B) trail frontier cloud models on complex reasoning.
Hardware is unavailable: 7B needs 8GB VRAM or RAM; 70B needs 48GB — check before planning.
Real-time latency requirements on small hardware — CPU inference is 5-20x slower than GPU.
Multilingual tasks across more than 20 languages — local models lag cloud providers significantly.

how-to.step: Install Ollama on Linux: curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
Start the server: ollama serve (runs on port 11434 by default)
Pull a model: ollama pull llama3.1:8b

list.item: DeepSeek Coder V2 (16B, 236B) — GPT-4 level coding

section.name: hardware-requirements
section.p: Minimum RAM and GPU VRAM by model size:

rule.statement: Match model size to available RAM/VRAM before pulling — loading a model that exceeds available memory causes OOM errors or falls back to CPU with unacceptable latency.
rule.rationale: A 70B model at Q4 quantization uses ~40GB; exceeding VRAM causes partial offload to RAM which is 5-20x slower.

rule.statement: Use quantized versions (Q4_K_M or Q5) for memory-constrained environments; accept the 15-30% quality trade-off consciously.
rule.rationale: Q4_K_M cuts memory usage roughly in half compared to full precision, enabling models to run on consumer GPUs.

rule.statement: Pin model tags explicitly: use llama3.1:8b not llama3.1 — untagged version may resolve to a different quantization after ollama pull.
rule.rationale: Untagged pulls may silently upgrade to a different quantization, changing response quality or failing to load on the same hardware.

rule.statement: Use dedicated embedding models (nomic-embed-text, mxbai-embed-large) for embedding tasks, not generalist chat models.
rule.rationale: Chat models produce lower-quality embeddings and use more VRAM than purpose-built embedding models.

rule.statement: Set OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=1h to avoid 5-30s cold start on each request when models are used frequently.
rule.rationale: Without keep-alive, Ollama unloads the model after each session, causing reload overhead on the next call.

list.item: Model won't load: check RAM, use smaller model or quantized version
Slow inference: enable GPU, check CUDA/Metal detection with nvidia-smi
Server not responding: run ollama serve, check port 11434 is free
Truncated output: increase num_ctx in options
OOM errors: reduce context window or batch size, use quantized model
Tool calling fails: use 32k+ context, Llama 3.1+ recommended
Tool/Function Calling — stable (requires Llama 3.1+ and 32k+ context)
Structured Outputs (JSON Schema) — stable (compliance varies by model)
